title: Update README, operator guidance, and migration notes for the in-repo live adapter
description:
Background
- Once the live adapter is in-repo, the README and operator guidance must stop implying that live mode is an external integration point.
- The migration also needs durable guidance for anyone who has been using the old worker config or operational habits.

Goal
- Update the docs and migration notes so they explain the new truth clearly and preserve honest scope language.

Scope
- README updates.
- Operator guidance.
- Migration from the external worker assumption.
- Explicit documentation of remaining blockers or deferred surfaces.
acceptance_criteria:
- The docs explain the supported in-repo live behavior without relying on unstated external modules.
- Migration guidance exists for current users of the external worker config or workflow.
- Deferred or blocked surfaces remain clearly documented instead of being implied away.
close_reason: Merged into canonical feature lw-m5k.7 during backlog consolidation

title: Normalize live chat payloads directly into the canonical intake, task, and render pipeline
description:
Background
- The project already has the canonical intake ledger, heuristic routing, task registry, runtime tracking, and note writer.
- The biggest architectural risk in chat integration is accidentally routing live polling back through the MVP's separate DB and note sync path.

Goal
- Normalize live chat payloads directly into the canonical pipeline so there is only one source of truth for raw capture, task generation, and rendering.

Scope
- Chat payload normalization.
- Checkpoint alignment with the current store.
- Prohibition on a second worker DB or direct MVP note writes.
acceptance_criteria:
- Live chat payloads flow into the canonical raw capture store instead of a second worker DB.
- Chat intake, classification, task creation, and rendering all run through the in-repo stack.
- The design explicitly prevents a fallback to MVP-specific note-writing behavior.
close_reason: Merged into canonical feature lw-m5k.3 during backlog consolidation

title: Run worker continuously with launchd
description: Run the worker continuously under launchd so ingestion no longer depends on manual one-shot commands.
close_reason: done

title: Make document and comment capability gaps explicit instead of silently collapsing scope
description:
Background
- The plan is clear that missing or blocked access must be visible rather than silently flattened away.
- Documents and comments are the most likely place for scope gaps, permission blockers, and partial support claims to get muddy.

Goal
- Make capability gaps for document-side live coverage explicit in the CLI, health reporting, and operator documentation.

Scope
- Unsupported-surface messaging.
- Permission blocker behavior.
- Honest reporting of deferred capability instead of silent omission.
acceptance_criteria:
- The system has an explicit operator-visible strategy for document-side blockers and deferrals.
- Missing permissions or unsupported retrieval paths are surfaced rather than silently treated as no-op success.
- The behavior is aligned with the rollout honesty rules defined earlier in the plan.
close_reason: Merged into canonical feature lw-m5k.5 during backlog consolidation

title: Capture the durable gap analysis between the external MVP and the project plan
description:
Background
- The current judgment that the MVP is useful but insufficient must survive beyond this conversation.
- Future implementation choices will be much safer if the gap analysis is durable and explicit rather than reconstructed from memory.

Goal
- Capture a self-contained analysis of what the MVP already proves, what it does not prove, and why the plan rejects a wholesale import.

Scope
- Enumerate the transport and operational pieces worth reusing.
- Enumerate the missing semantics that make the MVP insufficient as the final architecture.
- Tie the conclusion back to single-source-of-truth, vault-safety, replay, and provenance goals.
acceptance_criteria:
- The analysis explicitly names which MVP pieces are sufficient seeds for the in-repo adapter.
- The analysis explicitly names the missing or incompatible pieces that prevent whole-worker adoption.
- The write-up explains why the project plan and `AGENTS.md` require the canonical pipeline to stay in-repo.
close_reason: Merged into canonical feature lw-m5k.1 during backlog consolidation

title: Reimplement sync-once, backfill, and sync-daemon for chat mode on the internal service
description:
Background
- The CLI already promises `sync-once`, `backfill`, and `sync-daemon`, but the current implementation delegates those commands to the external worker.
- Chat self-containment is not real until the CLI live commands execute against the internal service and canonical runtime model.

Goal
- Reimplement the chat live commands on top of the internal service and remove the dynamic import boundary for the supported chat path.

Scope
- `sync-once`.
- `backfill`.
- `sync-daemon`.
- Removal of external-worker assumptions for chat mode.
acceptance_criteria:
- The supported chat live commands can run without an external `automation.lark_worker` module on the Python path.
- `sync-once`, `backfill`, and `sync-daemon` all use the same internal service and canonical runtime state.
- The CLI remains explicit about unsupported surfaces rather than implying full live parity too early.
close_reason: Merged into canonical feature lw-m5k.3 during backlog consolidation

title: Map every MVP worker module to a clear in-repo fate: port, adapt, replace, or reference-only
description:
Background
- The MVP contains multiple modules that blend transport, storage, distillation, rendering, source-access probing, and runtime operations.
- Porting selectively is only safe if each module has an explicit fate instead of an implicit assumption.

Goal
- Produce a module-by-module responsibility map that says whether each MVP piece should be ported, adapted, replaced, or kept only as reference material.

Scope
- Cover config, transport, service orchestration, DB, distillation, note sync, source-access probing, fixture generation, event handling, and launchd support.
- Record why each fate supports the overall project goals better than a wholesale import.
acceptance_criteria:
- Every meaningful MVP module has a stated fate: port, adapt, replace, or reference-only.
- The map makes clear that MVP storage, distillation, and note-writing logic are not authoritative for the in-repo runtime.
- The result is detailed enough that future implementation work does not need to reverse-engineer the migration boundary again.
close_reason: Merged into canonical feature lw-m5k.1 during backlog consolidation

title: Expand the multilingual fixture corpus plus failure-path, redaction, and negative e2e cases for live ingestion
description:
Background
- The plan requires failure-path, redaction, and sensitivity coverage plus a fixture corpus that actually exercises the source classes and lifecycle cases we claim to support.
- A fresh review of the graph shows that this bead should also be the explicit home for the representative multilingual and lifecycle-heavy fixture corpus that later unit, replay, and e2e checks will depend on.

Goal
- Expand the fixture corpus and the negative-path validation inputs so live adapter work is validated against realistic edge cases, multilingual samples, and operator-relevant failure scenarios.

Scope
- Fixture-corpus expansion across DMs, groups, documents, comments, and replies.
- English, Chinese, and mixed-language samples.
- Lifecycle cases such as edits, deletes, repeated asks, near-duplicates, and context-only examples.
- Failure-path, redaction, and negative end-to-end cases that should emit rich logs when they fail.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The fixture plan expands to cover every in-scope source surface plus multilingual and lifecycle-heavy examples.
2. Redaction and sensitive-source tests are explicitly included in scope.
3. Failure-path coverage is treated as a requirement, not a stretch goal.
4. Negative end-to-end cases are included so later scripted verification has realistic bad-path inputs and useful logs.
close_reason: Merged into canonical feature lw-m5k.7 during backlog consolidation

title: Port chat event subscription into the canonical pipeline and define dedup or coalescing with polling
description:
Background
- The plan requires event-driven and polled updates to converge on one logical intake stream.
- The MVP has a chat event listener, but it is not yet integrated into the canonical runtime or deduped against polling.

Goal
- Bring event-driven chat intake into the in-repo runtime and define the coalescing or dedup behavior needed to prevent double-processing.

Scope
- Event subscription.
- Shared intake path with polling.
- Coalescing or dedup rules for mixed live paths.
acceptance_criteria:
- Event-driven chat intake enters the same canonical path as polling.
- The design includes explicit dedup or coalescing rules for poll-plus-event overlap.
- The event path does not become a second independent source of truth.
close_reason: Chat events: im.message.receive_v1 NDJSON via live.chat_events into mixed ledger; sync-events CLI with coalesce/drain matching polling path; tests and README.

title: Add transport-facing mixed chat observation bridge for canonical ledger drain
description:
Background
- lw-8hx still covered too much transport scope after the mixed poll/event ledger and runtime drain landed.
- The remaining completed slice is narrower: a transport-facing helper that lets poll and event chat observations enter the same canonical observe-plus-drain path without creating a second source of truth.

Goal
- Capture the already-implemented bridge step as its own bead so it can be completed independently of later event-subscriber transport work.

Scope
- Add a transport-facing runtime helper that records one poll or event observation into the canonical chat-intake ledger.
- Opportunistically drain any now-ready canonical rows into the raw message store.
- Cover immediate poll ingestion plus pending-event-then-poll convergence with focused tests.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Transport callers have one in-tree helper for canonical observe-plus-drain behavior.
2. Poll and event observations converge on the same mixed-intake ledger and raw-capture path.
3. Focused validation covers immediate poll ingestion and pending-event-then-poll convergence.
close_reason: Completed by adding observe_and_drain_chat_message() plus focused runtime tests for immediate poll ingestion and pending-event-then-poll convergence.

title: Plan the canonical schema and repository changes needed for mutable document surfaces
description:
Background
- The current schema is still centered on `raw_messages` and does not yet carry the richer document-side semantics the plan calls for.
- The plan explicitly wants append-only raw history, current-state materialization, content-hash skip, supersession, and tombstones for mutable sources.

Goal
- Translate the richer document-side model into concrete schema and repository changes in the canonical store.

Scope
- Raw history representation.
- Current-state or latest-revision helpers.
- Content-hash skip, supersession, and tombstone handling.
acceptance_criteria:
- The schema plan covers append-only history plus whatever current-state helpers are needed for replay and reconciliation.
- Content-hash skip, supersession, and tombstone behavior are first-class design concerns rather than afterthoughts.
- The schema evolution plan stays compatible with the repo's canonical SQLite-first architecture.
close_reason: Merged into canonical feature lw-m5k.4 during backlog consolidation

title: Define metadata completeness and traceability fields across live records and surfaced tasks
description:
Background
- The plan includes a detailed metadata preservation contract: author, create and update times, source, stream, source class, external ID, parent anchors, canonical deep links, participant or watcher context, raw payload references, normalized excerpts, content hash, lifecycle state, intake path, and policy version.
- The current live graph covers ingest keys, ledger state, and document-side modeling, but it still does not isolate metadata completeness and traceability as its own cross-surface task.
- Without that explicit task, it is too easy for chat and document implementations to preserve slightly different subsets of the contract and only discover the gaps later during replay, debugging, or user-facing provenance work.

Goal
- Define the cross-surface metadata completeness and traceability contract for live-captured records and surfaced tasks.

Scope
- Required metadata fields across chat, document, comment, and reply records.
- Canonical deep links, participant or watcher context, raw payload references, normalized excerpts, and policy-version provenance.
- How surfaced tasks retain enough source metadata to remain auditable and debuggable.
- Boundaries between durable metadata, derived summaries, and operator-facing provenance views.

Why this matters
- This task makes the plan's traceability promises concrete and keeps provenance from becoming a hand-wavy aspiration.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The live design names the required metadata set across chat, document, comment, and reply records instead of leaving field completeness implicit.
2. Canonical deep links, participant or watcher context, raw payload references, normalized excerpts, and policy-version provenance are explicitly accounted for.
3. Surfaced tasks retain enough source metadata to remain auditable, replayable, and debuggable.
4. The metadata contract is precise enough to guide implementation and later validation.
close_reason: Merged into canonical feature lw-m5k.9 during backlog consolidation

title: Add retry, quarantine, auth guidance, and health metrics for the live runtime
description:
Background
- Live mode cannot be trustworthy if transient subprocess failures, malformed payloads, or auth expiry turn into silent data loss or whole-run collapse.
- The plan explicitly calls for retries, quarantine, health metrics, and clear operator guidance. After a fresh review, this bead also needs to be explicit about operator-grade diagnostics so failures are not only bounded but understandable.

Goal
- Define and implement the live runtime safeguards that keep failures observable, bounded, and diagnosable.

Scope
- Retry and backoff.
- Quarantine or dead-letter behavior.
- Auth-expiry guidance.
- Live-mode health metrics relevant to trust and operability.
- Structured run logging and stage-level diagnostics sufficient to explain why a run succeeded, degraded, or failed.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Live mode has an explicit strategy for retries, permanent-failure quarantine, and auth guidance.
2. The health model includes the kinds of signals the plan cares about, such as lag, duplicates, error rate, or capability blockers.
3. Failure handling preserves replayability and operator trust rather than hiding problems.
4. Detailed structured diagnostics make it possible to trace failure mode, stage, and relevant run context without guesswork.
close_reason: Implemented runtime safeguards with duplicate-pressure health metrics, structured runtime diagnostics in doctor output, and stage-aware auth/runtime error classification plus recovery actions in live CLI paths.

title: Define rollout gates and honest scope language for chat-first self-containment versus full source parity
description:
Background
- The project can legitimately ship a chat-first self-contained live mode before full document-surface parity, but only if the boundaries are explicit and honest.
- Ambiguous partial support would violate the repo's trust model and create long-term confusion.

Goal
- Define rollout gates that separate acceptable phase-one chat self-containment from later document, comment, and reply parity.

Scope
- Phase definitions.
- Exit criteria.
- Honest operator-facing language for supported, blocked, and deferred surfaces.
acceptance_criteria:
- The rollout plan defines a chat-first phase with explicit boundaries and success conditions.
- The rollout plan defines what must be true before document, comment, and reply coverage can be claimed.
- The guidance includes an explicit rule against overstating unsupported live coverage in docs or CLI messaging.
close_reason: Merged into canonical feature lw-m5k.1 during backlog consolidation

title: Expand watched-source governance to cover pause or resume, backfill windows, ignore rules, and redaction hooks
description:
Background
- The plan requires more governance richness than the MVP currently models, including pause or resume semantics, configurable backfill windows, ignore rules, and redaction hooks.
- Without these fields, the live adapter cannot satisfy the plan's source governance and trust requirements.

Goal
- Translate the governance expectations from the plan into concrete in-repo source and policy fields for live ingestion.

Scope
- Pause and resume behavior.
- Backfill window policy.
- Ignore and redaction hooks.
- Explicit alignment with watched-source governance in the canonical store.
acceptance_criteria:
- The planned governance model covers pause or resume, backfill windows, ignore rules, and redaction hooks.
- The bead explains how these controls interact with live polling, backfill, replay, and rendering.
- The governance design is consistent with the repo's single-source-of-truth and vault-safety goals.
close_reason: Merged into canonical feature lw-m5k.2 during backlog consolidation

title: Define governance, sensitivity, and reconciliation semantics for document-side live ingestion
description:
Background
- Document surfaces are where governance, sensitivity, and reconciliation questions become much more complex than simple chat polling.
- The plan requires explicit watched-source governance, visible capability blockers, and later repair or replay behavior that is still trustworthy.

Goal
- Define how governance, redaction, and reconciliation should behave once document surfaces enter the live adapter.

Scope
- Watched-document policy.
- Sensitive-source and redaction implications.
- Reconcile and repair expectations for mutable document-side streams.
acceptance_criteria:
- The design explains how watched documents fit into the same governance framework as chats without flattening away their differences.
- The bead captures the redaction and sensitivity implications of durable document capture.
- Reconciliation expectations for document-side streams are explicit enough to guide later runtime work.
close_reason: Merged into canonical feature lw-m5k.4 during backlog consolidation

title: Port document fetch, comment, and reply transport wrappers into the in-repo intake layer
description:
Background
- The MVP already knows useful `lark-cli` commands for docs search and fetch plus comment and reply listing, but that logic is stranded outside this repo.
- The first step toward canonical document ingestion is to port the transport knowledge itself into the in-repo intake layer.

Goal
- Move document, comment, and reply transport wrappers into `src/lark_to_notes` so later normalization work has a canonical place to live.

Scope
- Docs search and fetch.
- Comment listing.
- Reply listing.
- Structured error handling and visibility behavior for these surfaces.
acceptance_criteria:
- The in-repo intake layer owns the document, comment, and reply transport wrappers.
- The transport design captures the actual `lark-cli` access paths already proven by the MVP.
- Transport failures are represented clearly enough to feed the capability and blocker model.
close_reason: Merged into canonical feature lw-m5k.5 during backlog consolidation

title: Add end-to-end verification scripts with detailed structured logging and artifact capture for live sync
description:
Background
- The user asked explicitly that these beads include comprehensive end-to-end test scripts with great, detailed logging so implementation can be verified confidently after the fact.
- The current validation graph now covers baseline unit and integration checks, rendering-focused suites, feedback-focused suites, and ops-focused suites, but it still needs one explicit bead for operator-style end-to-end scripts that tie those pieces together.

Goal
- Add end-to-end verification scripts for live sync with detailed structured logging, preserved artifacts, and operator-readable outcomes.

Scope
- Chat-first end-to-end verification flows.
- Document, comment, and reply verification flows where supported.
- Replay and reconcile verification flows.
- Structured logs, preserved artifacts, and clear pass or fail summaries suitable for diagnosing real implementation regressions.

Why this matters
- This bead turns the validation strategy into something an operator or future agent can actually run to answer the question: does the whole live pipeline work end to end, and if not, where did it fail?
acceptance_criteria:
1. The backlog includes explicit end-to-end verification scripts for chat, document-side, replay, and reconcile flows where support exists.
2. These scripts are expected to emit detailed structured logs and preserve useful failure artifacts rather than only returning a pass or fail code.
3. The scripts produce operator-readable summaries that make it obvious which stage failed and what evidence to inspect next.
4. The e2e layer complements rather than replaces unit, integration, replay, and golden tests elsewhere in the graph.
notes:
Future-self reminder
- If implementation only adds unit tests and never produces runnable end-to-end scripts with rich logs, this bead is not done.
close_reason: Merged into canonical feature lw-m5k.7 during backlog consolidation

title: Implement an internal lark-cli client for DM and group polling with structured error mapping
description:
Background
- The MVP already proves some working `lark-cli` command patterns for DM and group polling, but that knowledge currently lives outside the repo.
- The in-repo adapter needs its own transport layer with error mapping, auth guidance, and testable interfaces.

Goal
- Port the chat polling transport knowledge into `src/lark_to_notes` in a way that supports the canonical runtime and operator experience.

Scope
- DM and group polling commands.
- Structured error mapping.
- Clear recovery guidance for auth and visibility problems.
acceptance_criteria:
- The in-repo transport layer can poll DM and group sources without importing the external worker.
- Command errors are translated into operator-meaningful failures rather than opaque subprocess noise.
- Auth and visibility failures have explicit guidance consistent with the CLI's operator contract.
close_reason: Merged into canonical feature lw-m5k.3 during backlog consolidation

title: Build a self-contained in-repo live adapter layer that replaces external automation.lark_worker
description:
Background
- The current CLI exposes live commands such as `sync-once`, `sync-daemon`, `backfill`, and live `reconcile`, but they dynamically import `automation.lark_worker` from outside this checkout.
- The project plan explicitly says the MVP worker is a non-normative reference only. The in-repo Python runtime and SQLite store are supposed to be the canonical implementation.
- Assessment of the MVP shows that it is useful as a transport/reference layer for DM/group polling, event subscription, docs/comments command discovery, fixture probing, and some launchd helpers, but not sufficient as the final live adapter for this project.

Why this epic exists
- Porting the whole worker unchanged would reintroduce a second state database, a second note writer, a second distillation stack, and weaker semantics than the plan requires.
- Ignoring the MVP entirely would throw away useful operator knowledge about real `lark-cli` commands and working retrieval paths.
- The correct approach is to absorb the useful source-access and transport knowledge into `src/lark_to_notes`, then feed the existing canonical intake, task, runtime, budget, and render layers.

Scope
- Make live mode self-contained in this repository for supported surfaces.
- Keep this repo's SQLite schema and writer pipeline as the only source of truth.
- Port or redesign only the MVP pieces that help satisfy the plan and `AGENTS.md`.
- Make unsupported or blocked surfaces explicit instead of silently claiming parity.
design:
Expanded design frame
- The migration away from automation.lark_worker is not only a transport port. It is a contract-preservation exercise across governance, shared intake state, task identity, review visibility, safe vault rendering, runtime behavior, and operational controls.
- The live adapter must remain aligned with the canonical in-repo SQLite store, the canonical task system, and the machine-owned Markdown rendering model.
- The backlog is therefore organized so future implementation can move from migration boundary and governance, through intake semantics, into task meaning and vault visibility, and finally into runtime hardening, cost control, validation, and operator documentation.

Interpretation guidance
- Chat-first self-containment is still a valid rollout gate, but only if the missing document, review-lane, rendering, and operational contracts are made explicit rather than implied away.
- Any later attempt to collapse these tracks back into one generic sync task should be treated as a warning sign that the plan is losing important safety detail.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Live mode no longer depends on an external automation.lark_worker import for supported surfaces.
2. The backlog explicitly covers governance, shared intake-ledger semantics, task identity and review-lane behavior, vault-safe rendering and promotion, operational controls, validation, and operator documentation.
3. Chat and document live paths remain tied to the canonical in-repo SQLite, task, runtime, and note-writing stack instead of a second worker database or note writer.
4. Unsupported, blocked, or deferred surfaces remain visible to operators rather than being silently flattened out of scope.
5. The resulting bead graph is detailed enough that future implementation work does not need to reconstruct the core live-sync design intent from memory.
notes:
Future-self reminder
- Do not confuse transport completion with product completion. In this project, live sync is only real when the trust boundary survives contact with continuous operation.
close_reason: All lw-m5k.1 through lw-m5k.11 blockers completed; in-repo ChatLiveAdapter and canonical SQLite replace automation.lark_worker import; document adapters in-tree; epic scope satisfied.

title: Codify migration boundary, architectural decision, and rollout gates for live adapter integration
description:
Background
- We now have a clear assessment that the external worker is useful but insufficient. That judgment needs to become durable project knowledge rather than context trapped in a single chat.
- The plan explicitly prioritizes provenance, canonical updates, replayability, and vault safety over convenience.

Goal
- Turn the migration reasoning into explicit architecture and rollout decisions so future implementation does not drift toward a wholesale import of the MVP or make misleading claims about live coverage.

Scope
- Capture the gap analysis.
- Map which worker responsibilities are ported, redesigned, or dropped.
- Define phase gates and honest operator-facing scope language.
acceptance_criteria:
- The repo has a durable written decision about what the MVP is and is not good for.
- Each major worker responsibility is explicitly classified as port, adapt, replace, or reference-only.
- Rollout gates make it clear what counts as acceptable chat-only self-containment versus full source-class parity.
close_reason: Codified the migration boundary, worker-fate mapping, and honest rollout gates for the in-repo live adapter in the normative plan.

title: Preserve vault-safe raw, daily, and Current Tasks rendering behavior in live sync
description:
Background
- The live-sync backlog currently assumes that once records enter the canonical pipeline, the existing renderer will somehow preserve all of the vault contract requirements automatically. The plan does not allow that assumption.
- This repository is not just a codebase; it is a live notes vault with explicit rules about `raw/` first, `daily/` first, promotion into `area/current tasks/index.md`, backlinking, opt-in durable-note updates, and machine-owned block safety.
- A live adapter that writes notes without an explicit rendering and promotion contract would be one of the highest-risk ways to damage trust, because the failures would show up as duplicate notes, misplaced tasks, unsafe rewrites, or subtle erosion of user-authored context.
- The existing live-adapter beads need a dedicated place where future selves can answer: which note gets written first, which surfaces are authoritative, how are stable block IDs preserved, and where do automation boundaries intentionally stop?

Goal
- Make the vault-safe rendering, promotion, backlink, and machine-owned-block contract explicit for live-created artifacts so the trust boundary is preserved under real-time operation.

Scope
- Raw-first rendering for live-captured source material.
- Daily-note insertion and review-lane behavior.
- Current Tasks promotion, merge, and backlink rules.
- Safe boundaries for people, project, and area note updates.
- Stable machine-owned sections and protection of user-authored content during rerenders.

Why this is a separate feature track
- The renderer already exists, but live sync changes the operational pressure on it: records arrive incrementally, may be revised, may recur, and may need bounded updates without clobbering nearby human edits.
- Treating note-writing as merely an implementation detail of ingestion is exactly how unsafe automation sneaks in.
- Future selves should read this feature as the durable statement of how live sync remains a vault-safe notes workflow rather than becoming a generic content mirror.
close_reason: All child beads lw-m5k.10.1–10.5 closed; plan now covers live raw-first, durable-note boundary, daily insertion, Current Tasks promotion, and rerender-safe machine blocks.

title: Define the raw-first live rendering contract for chat and document source notes
description:
Background
- The vault contract says new source material should land in `raw/` first, and that raw notes are the first visible capture surface derived from the canonical raw store.
- The existing live-adapter graph does not yet say how live-captured chat and document records become raw notes or context artifacts without duplicating notes, misplacing files, or bypassing metadata expectations.
- If live sync writes raw material inconsistently, the rest of the vault workflow becomes harder to reason about because source capture no longer has a predictable home.

Goal
- Define the raw-first rendering contract for live-captured source material across supported surfaces.

Scope
- Placement rules for raw notes or raw context artifacts.
- Surface-specific expectations for chat versus document-derived raw capture.
- Metadata and backlink expectations for raw artifacts.
- How raw rendering relates to the canonical database-backed raw store rather than bypassing it.

Why this matters
- This task preserves the project's rule that raw capture is visible, inspectable, and ordered before curation.
close_reason: Added Live Raw-First Rendering Contract (chat/document) in plan_for_lark_to_notes_workflow.md

title: Define live daily-note insertion and stable managed sections for open tasks and review candidates
description:
Background
- The plan says distilled action items should land in the relevant daily note first and that `needs-review` items should remain visible there rather than jumping straight into durable Current Tasks.
- The current live graph does not yet isolate the exact daily-note update contract for incremental live arrivals, rerenders, or repeated sightings.
- This is dangerous because daily notes are where the system first becomes user-visible as curated work rather than raw capture.

Goal
- Define the live daily-note update contract, including managed sections for open tasks and review candidates.

Scope
- Which daily note a live item belongs to.
- Stable managed section structure for open tasks and review candidates.
- Incremental update behavior when the same live task is seen again or revised.
- Preservation of nearby user-authored daily-note content.

Why this matters
- This task translates the daily-first workflow from the plan into concrete live note-writing behavior.
close_reason: Live Daily-Note Insertion Contract in plan_for_lark_to_notes_workflow.md

title: Define Current Tasks promotion, merge, and backlink rules for live-created tasks
description:
Background
- The plan says still-open work should be promoted into `area/current tasks/index.md`, that duplicate tasks should merge by stable identity, and that open tasks should retain backlinks to source and daily-note context.
- The current live graph lacks a dedicated bead for how promotion into Current Tasks works under incremental live updates.
- Without this, live sync could either leave durable work stranded in daily notes forever or over-promote noisy or duplicate items into the durable work view.

Goal
- Define the Current Tasks promotion, merge, persistence, and backlink rules for live-created tasks.

Scope
- Promotion triggers from daily-note items into Current Tasks.
- Merge behavior when a live task already exists in the durable list.
- Backlinks to raw and daily-note context.
- Expectations for how long promoted items remain durable until manually updated or later lifecycle logic exists.

Why this matters
- This task protects the durable open-work view from both under-promotion and duplicate pollution.
close_reason: Live Current Tasks Promotion Contract in plan_for_lark_to_notes_workflow.md

title: Keep people, project, and area note updates manual or opt-in until core live surfaces are proven safe
description:
Background
- The plan and `AGENTS.md` both say broader updates into `people/`, `projects/`, and `area/` notes should remain manual or opt-in until the safer raw, daily, and Current Tasks surfaces are proven stable.
- The current live graph references honest scope but does not yet have a dedicated bead that captures this durable-note boundary as a design rule.
- This matters because once live sync starts writing beyond the core machine-owned surfaces, the risk of creating duplicate durable notes or distorting human-maintained knowledge increases sharply.

Goal
- Make the live durable-note boundary explicit so broader vault automation remains manual or opt-in until core safety is proven.

Scope
- People notes.
- Project notes.
- Area notes outside Current Tasks.
- Operator messaging for what remains intentionally out of automatic scope.

Why this matters
- This task preserves the repo's stated trust boundary instead of letting it erode through incremental feature creep.
close_reason: Added Live Durable-Note Boundary subsection under Note Rendering Contract in plan_for_lark_to_notes_workflow.md

title: Enforce stable machine-owned block IDs and preserve user-authored content during live rerenders
description:
Background
- The machine-owned block pattern is one of the main safeguards that lets automation coexist with human-authored Markdown safely.
- The current live graph mentions machine-owned sections at a high level, but it does not yet isolate the live-specific requirement to preserve stable block IDs and protect nearby user content during incremental rerenders.
- This matters even more in live mode than replay mode because records may arrive repeatedly, partially, or after user edits have already occurred around the managed blocks.

Goal
- Enforce stable machine-owned block IDs and preservation of user-authored content during live rerenders.

Scope
- Stable per-section and per-item IDs.
- Replacement rules for incremental rerenders.
- Boundaries between machine-owned and human-authored content.
- Expectations for how live rerenders handle reordered, updated, or removed managed items.

Why this matters
- This task is the explicit safety valve that prevents live note updates from becoming unsafe Markdown rewrites.
close_reason: Live Rerender Safety for Machine-Owned Blocks in plan_for_lark_to_notes_workflow.md

title: Add live cost, performance, and single-writer safety controls
description:
Background
- The plan does not define live sync success purely as feature parity. It also requires the system to remain operable, observable, cost-aware, and safe under continuous use.
- The current live-adapter graph has runtime hardening tasks, but it still does not give budget accounting, large-source performance controls, or the one-serialized-writer guarantee a dedicated planning home.
- Those omissions matter because live sync is exactly where costs, noisy sources, bursty updates, and concurrent note-write pressure show up first. If they are not planned explicitly, they get bolted on after trust has already been eroded.
- This feature exists so future selves can see that `works live` means more than `polls successfully`; it must also mean bounded cost, bounded write concurrency, and predictable behavior under load.

Goal
- Add explicit cost, throughput, and single-writer safety controls around live sync so the in-repo adapter remains sustainable in everyday operation.

Scope
- LLM budget accounting and heuristics-only fallback expectations in live runs.
- Chunking, batching, caching, coalescing, and backpressure for high-volume or large-document ingestion.
- Enforcement of one serialized note writer and locking semantics around live-driven writes.
- Operational metrics needed to observe latency, duplicate pressure, blocker states, and budget burn.

Why this is a separate feature track
- The migration away from the external worker is not complete if the new path is technically canonical but operationally fragile.
- Budget, load-shedding, and write-serialization are not nice-to-haves for this project; they are part of the trust model in both `AGENTS.md` and the plan.
- Future selves should read this track as the answer to: `How does live sync stay safe and sustainable after day two, not just during a demo run?`
close_reason: lw-m5k.11.1–11.3 closed; plan covers live budget, throughput, and serialized writer locking.

title: Define live LLM-budget accounting, provider-routing, and heuristics-only fallback behavior
description:
Background
- The plan requires call count, token count, duration, budget consumption, cache hit rate, fallback count, and graceful degradation when provider limits or pricing pressure make LLM usage undesirable.
- The existing live-adapter graph talks about runtime health, but it does not yet isolate the live-specific budget and fallback contract.
- That gap matters because live sync is the mode most likely to accumulate small but frequent model calls over time, turning an apparently cheap workflow into an operational surprise.

Goal
- Define the live-run budget-accounting, provider-routing, and heuristics-only fallback behavior for the in-repo adapter.

Scope
- Which live stages may call LLMs.
- What budget metrics must be tracked during live runs.
- How live mode degrades into heuristics-only behavior when budgets or provider access are unavailable.
- Operator-visible reporting for budget pressure and skipped model usage.

Why this matters
- This task turns cost-awareness from a plan principle into an operational live-sync rule set.
close_reason: Plan contract added in plan_for_lark_to_notes_workflow.md (lw-m5k.11.1)

title: Define chunking, batching, caching, backpressure, and load-shedding controls for live sync
description:
Background
- The plan calls out coalescing, batching, caching, and large-document chunking as part of runtime maturation, and it also requires per-run controls that prevent accidental overload.
- The current live graph covers retries and event dedup, but it does not yet isolate the broader throughput-control contract for large or noisy sources.
- If this remains implicit, the system may be functionally correct on small fixtures but brittle under realistic live load.

Goal
- Define the throughput-control strategy for large, bursty, or noisy live sources.

Scope
- Chunking for large documents or high-context inputs.
- Batching, caching, and coalescing of repeated work.
- Backpressure and load-shedding behavior when live input outpaces downstream stages.
- Interaction between throughput controls and governance caps.

Why this matters
- This task is where live sync becomes something the operator can actually leave running instead of babysitting constantly.
close_reason: Plan contract added in plan_for_lark_to_notes_workflow.md (lw-m5k.11.2)

title: Preserve one serialized note writer and locking semantics under mixed live update paths
description:
Background
- The project architecture calls for one serialized note writer, and `AGENTS.md` explicitly expects locking and single-writer tests once implementation exists.
- The current live graph mentions single-writer safety in passing, but it does not yet isolate the actual live-write concurrency contract.
- This is a critical trust issue because mixed poll, event, replay, and repair paths all create pressure to write notes concurrently unless the design makes serialization explicit.

Goal
- Preserve the one-serialized-writer and locking contract under live-driven note updates.

Scope
- The note-write serialization model.
- Locking boundaries around live note updates.
- Interaction between polling, events, replay, and reconcile repair.
- Failure behavior when the writer is busy or a lock cannot be acquired safely.

Why this matters
- This task is the concrete planning answer to the question `how do we prevent live sync from racing its own note writes?`
close_reason: Plan contract added in plan_for_lark_to_notes_workflow.md (lw-m5k.11.3)

title: Define canonical live config and watched-source governance for the in-repo adapter
description:
Background
- The old worker has a simple JSON config and an operator workflow people may already rely on.
- The current repo has a more canonical watched-source model and stronger expectations around governance, safety, and replay.

Goal
- Preserve compatibility where it helps adoption, but move governance authority into the in-repo model rather than keeping worker-specific config as the source of truth.

Scope
- Config compatibility.
- Watched-source governance enrichment.
- Surface capability reporting and blocker visibility.
acceptance_criteria:
- There is a clear contract for how old worker-style config maps into the in-repo source model.
- Governance features required by the plan are explicitly represented or intentionally deferred with rationale.
- Operators can tell which source classes and retrieval paths are supported, blocked, or deferred.
close_reason: All lw-m5k.2.x blockers closed; plan covers governance, caps, document resolution, search rules, and policy versioning.

title: Model watched-document selection by explicit link, stable ID, and name-based discovery
description:
Background
- The plan explicitly says the user may identify relevant documents by link or by name, and that watched sources should be modeled as explicit allowlists rather than loose scraping rules.
- The current live-adapter graph talks about docs transport and capability blockers, but it does not yet spell out how a document moves from operator intent into a canonical watched-source record.
- If this remains implicit, different parts of the system may resolve the same document differently, especially when links, names, aliases, or search results drift over time.

Goal
- Define the canonical watched-document selection and resolution rules for live sync.

Scope
- Explicit document links and stable doc-token or doc-ID resolution.
- Name-based discovery and the rules for handling ambiguity, multiple hits, or renamed docs.
- How selected documents become authoritative watched-source entries in the in-repo model.
- Operator-visible feedback when a selection is lossy, ambiguous, or no longer resolvable.

Why this matters
- This is the front door for document-side governance. If selection semantics are fuzzy, every later document bead inherits that fuzziness.
close_reason: Defined deterministic watched-document selection semantics for explicit link/ID and name-search discovery, including ambiguity handling, canonical token materialization, and unresolved/blocked operator-visible states.

title: Add per-run and per-source ingestion caps plus flood-protection semantics to live governance
description:
Background
- The plan requires per-run and per-source caps so the system cannot accidentally flood itself with an unexpectedly large backlog or a noisy watched source.
- The current governance beads cover pause or resume, backfill windows, ignore rules, and redaction hooks, but they do not yet isolate flood-protection as a first-class rule set.
- That gap matters because live sync is exactly where a bad config, a newly added broad source, or a document with a large comment history could produce unsafe ingestion volume before an operator notices.

Goal
- Define the ingestion-cap and flood-protection semantics that keep live sync bounded and operator-friendly.

Scope
- Per-run caps.
- Per-source caps.
- How caps interact with backfill, incremental sync, retries, and deferred backlog.
- Operator-visible reporting when a cap is hit so truncation is explicit rather than silent.

Why this matters
- Caps are not just performance tuning. They are part of the governance and trust model that keeps the system conservative under uncertainty.
close_reason: Defined explicit per-run and per-source intake cap semantics, deferred-backlog behavior, retry/cap interaction, and operator-visible flood-protection diagnostics in the governance contract.

title: Version governance and policy inputs so live behavior stays explainable across replay and reclassification
description:
Background
- The plan says governance configuration should be versioned so later behavior can be traced back to the governing rules that produced it.
- Without explicit policy versioning, replay and reclassification can tell us that behavior changed, but not which rule-set change caused it.
- That makes future debugging and operator trust much worse, especially once live sync starts evolving heuristics, caps, ignore rules, or redaction behavior over time.

Goal
- Make governance and policy inputs versioned and traceable in the live adapter design.

Scope
- Version identifiers for watched-source and policy inputs.
- How policy versions attach to intake, rendering, and downstream derived artifacts.
- Expectations for replay and reclassification when the governing rules have changed.
- Operator-facing explanations of which policy version produced a given live outcome.

Why this matters
- This task is the bridge between governance design and auditability. It ensures live behavior stays explainable as the system matures.
close_reason: Governance and Policy Versioning for Live Explainability in plan_for_lark_to_notes_workflow.md

title: Model search-rule-based watched sources and result-stability semantics for live governance
description:
Background
- The plan says every watched source should be explicitly allowlisted by chat, document, or search rule. The current live-sync graph covers chats, explicit document selection, and capability visibility, but it still does not give search-rule-based watching a first-class design task.
- That omission matters because search rules are one of the easiest ways to introduce unstable scope, accidental over-ingestion, or ambiguous source resolution if they are treated like a casual convenience instead of a governed source type.

Goal
- Define how search-rule-based watched sources are represented, resolved, stabilized, and reviewed in the in-repo live adapter.

Scope
- Search-rule identity and persistence.
- How a search rule resolves to concrete watched sources over time.
- Ambiguity, result drift, and operator review when the set of matching sources changes.
- Governance boundaries so search rules remain explicit allowlists rather than loose scraping behavior.

Why this matters
- This task closes a real scope gap in the current beads: without it, one of the source governance modes named in the plan is still effectively implicit.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Search-rule-based watched sources are represented as an explicit governed source type rather than an ad hoc discovery shortcut.
2. The design explains how result drift, ambiguity, and changing match sets are surfaced to operators.
3. Search rules remain compatible with allowlist-first governance, caps, and replay-safe tracing.
4. The result is detailed enough to guide implementation without re-opening the basic semantics of search-based watching.
close_reason: Search-Rule Watched Sources and Result Stability in plan_for_lark_to_notes_workflow.md

title: Port chat transport and route live chat ingestion through the canonical in-repo pipeline
description:
Background
- The MVP's strongest implemented live behavior is DM and group polling plus some event support.
- The current repo already has the canonical intake, heuristics, task registry, runtime tracking, and writer pipeline that should own downstream behavior.

Goal
- Make chat live mode self-contained inside this repo and route it through the canonical pipeline instead of the MVP's storage and note-writing path.

Scope
- Internal `lark-cli` transport for DM and group polling.
- Normalization into the current intake contract.
- Canonical implementations of `sync-once`, `backfill`, and `sync-daemon` for chat sources.
acceptance_criteria:
- DM and group live polling no longer depends on importing the external worker.
- Chat live runs write into the canonical SQLite store and use the in-repo task/render pipeline.
- The chat live commands remain honest about what they do and do not support.
close_reason: Chat live self-contained: lark-cli transport, canonical intake/drain, CLI wired; ruff/mypy/pytest green.

title: Extend the canonical schema and normalized source model for revision-bearing document surfaces
description:
Background
- The plan requires documents, comments, and replies to be treated as mutable, revision-bearing records rather than static text blobs.
- The current schema and message model are still message-centric and not rich enough to capture the full document-side lifecycle safely.

Goal
- Design and land the richer canonical source model and storage semantics that document surfaces need before live adapter work claims parity.

Scope
- Normalized record model for docs, comments, and replies.
- Schema and repository evolution.
- Content-hash, supersession, and tombstone semantics for mutable source material.
acceptance_criteria:
- The in-repo canonical model can represent document content, comments, and replies without pretending they are ordinary chat messages.
- Raw capture and current-state semantics are compatible with revisions, edits, and deletes.
- Governance and reconciliation implications for document surfaces are spelled out before implementation proceeds.
close_reason: Schema v8 document_intake_ledger, DocumentIntakeItem model, observe/list/mark ledger API, tests

title: Implement in-repo live adapters for documents, comments, and replies
description:
Background
- The MVP already knows useful `lark-cli` commands for docs fetch, comments, and replies, but it does not turn them into a canonical live ingestion path.
- Full project scope requires more than probing or fixture capture. It requires stable normalization, incremental sync semantics, and explicit handling of capability gaps.

Goal
- Turn document, comment, and reply access knowledge into real in-repo live adapters that feed the canonical pipeline.

Scope
- Transport wrappers.
- Normalization and sync semantics.
- Explicit operator handling for blocked or unsupported document surfaces.
acceptance_criteria:
- Document fetch and comment/reply retrieval live in the in-repo intake layer rather than only in the external MVP.
- Document-side live ingestion has clear normalization and sync semantics.
- Capability blockers are surfaced explicitly instead of silently collapsing scope.
close_reason: In-repo lark-cli wrapper (JSON stderr/stdout), poll_document_surfaces_to_ledger for docs+comments+replies into document_intake_ledger, explicit wiki/file_type blockers and LarkCliApiError surfacing; tests with fake runner.

title: Add event-driven intake and runtime hardening around the in-repo live adapter
description:
Background
- The plan requires mixed polling and event-driven updates to converge on one logical intake stream, with retries, reconciliation, single-writer safety, and operator-visible health.
- The MVP has some event and launchd behavior, but it is not wired into the canonical runtime model.

Goal
- Rebuild the runtime side of live mode around the in-repo adapter so that event intake, retries, reconciliation, and background execution all serve the canonical pipeline.

Scope
- Event subscription.
- Dedup and coalescing with polling.
- Retry, quarantine, health metrics, launch/background runtime, and reconcile repair logic.
acceptance_criteria:
- Event-driven intake uses the same canonical path as polling instead of creating a parallel behavior stack.
- Live mode has clear retry, quarantine, auth-guidance, and health behaviors.
- Background execution and reconciliation are framed around the in-repo adapter, not the old worker state mirror.
close_reason: Live path: sync-events drain now uses RetryPolicy (retries/quarantine via execute_work_batch). doctor --json adds chat_intake_ledger counts (pending ready vs coalescing vs processed). sync-events --json emits runtime health + drain_batch run summary. Tests updated (intake counts, CLI, e2e doctor schema).

title: Design the operator-facing status, doctor, and inspect command surface for live sync
description:
Background
- The plan calls out an explicit operator CLI surface, and the current live beads cover core sync commands plus some health and documentation work, but they still do not isolate the operator-facing inspectability surface as its own task.
- Users will need more than sync-once and daemon commands. They will need ways to inspect watched sources, capability state, cursor progress, recent runs, blocker conditions, and budget or throttling status without spelunking the database.
- If this remains implicit, implementation is likely to undershoot user experience by treating CLI ergonomics as docs polish instead of a core operability surface.

Goal
- Define the operator-facing status, doctor, and inspect command surface for the live adapter.

Scope
- Commands or subcommands for watched-source inspection, capability or blocker state, cursor and backlog visibility, recent run summaries, and budget or throttle state.
- How these commands summarize degraded or partial support honestly.
- Boundaries between human-readable summaries and machine-parseable diagnostics.
- Relationship to existing sync, replay, reconcile, and health behaviors.

Why this matters
- This task improves real user operability by making the system explain itself without requiring code archaeology or direct database inspection.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The live roadmap includes an explicit operator-facing CLI surface beyond the raw sync commands.
2. Users can inspect watched-source state, capability or blocker status, cursor or backlog progress, recent run state, and budget or throttle conditions without database spelunking.
3. The design makes degraded or partial support visible rather than hiding it behind generic success output.
4. The command surface is detailed enough to guide both implementation and later operator documentation.
close_reason: Operator status/inspect/doctor CLI surface in plan_for_lark_to_notes_workflow.md

title: Add comprehensive validation coverage and migration docs for live adapter integration
description:
Background
- The plan and AGENTS.md are explicit that this project lives or dies by correctness, replayability, safe rendering, and honest scope. That means the migration work needs first-class testing and documentation, not just implementation beads.
- After a fresh review of the open graph, the biggest remaining planning risk is not missing transport work but under-specifying how validation should prove that live sync is trustworthy in real operator use.
- Future implementation should not have to guess whether unit tests are enough, whether end-to-end scripts are required, or whether detailed structured logging belongs in the validation strategy. This feature should answer those questions directly.

Goal
- Ensure the live adapter integration is covered by comprehensive unit, integration, replay, rendering, and end-to-end verification work, plus operator docs that explain the real supported behavior.

Scope
- Unit and integration validation for adapters, normalization, task identity, and rendering primitives.
- Replay/idempotency, failure-path, redaction, locking, and performance-control coverage.
- End-to-end verification scripts with detailed structured logging and artifact capture.
- README, migration docs, and operator guidance that reflect the truth.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The live adapter roadmap includes explicit unit tests, integration tests, replay checks, golden rendering checks, and end-to-end verification scripts rather than relying on smoke coverage.
2. Validation work explicitly includes detailed structured logging and artifact capture so failures are diagnosable after implementation.
3. Fixture coverage expands to match the planned source surfaces, lifecycle cases, and sensitive-source scenarios.
4. Operator docs and migration notes describe the actual supported behavior without relying on hidden context.
close_reason: README: live validation matrix fix, migration from automation/lark_worker, verify_live_adapter --artifacts-dir NDJSON; integration test for artifacts; all child beads and live prerequisites already closed.

title: Add live rendering and promotion tests for raw, daily, Current Tasks, and machine-owned-block safety
description:
Background
- The plan and `AGENTS.md` require golden-file tests for raw, daily, and Current Tasks rendering, and they treat rendering safety as part of the trust boundary rather than a cosmetic concern.
- The current validation beads mention golden rendering generally, but they do not yet isolate the live-specific rendering and promotion contract as its own test target.
- That gap matters because live sync introduces incremental rerenders, repeated sightings, and managed-block updates that can break differently from offline one-shot rendering.

Goal
- Add validation coverage for live raw-note, daily-note, and Current Tasks rendering behavior, including machine-owned-block safety.

Scope
- Raw rendering tests.
- Daily-note update tests.
- Current Tasks promotion and merge tests.
- Managed-block replacement and preservation-of-user-content tests.

Why this matters
- This task is where the rendering contract becomes something we can verify repeatedly instead of merely intending to preserve.
close_reason: Added NoteWriter pipeline tests for live-style incremental rerender: user-authored tails preserved on raw, daily, and Current Tasks when summary changes; promotion escalation from daily_only to current_tasks preserves prior tails and creates stable ltn-ct block.

title: Add task-identity, review-lane, and feedback-workflow tests for live sync
description:
Background
- The live-sync backlog now has explicit design work for confidence bands, task identity, review-lane behavior, and plannotator feedback flow, but those behaviors also need dedicated validation.
- A careful review against the plan shows one important gap: validation must not stop at generic feedback ingestion. It also needs to prove that structured triage actions and manual overrides round-trip cleanly and remain stable across replay.

Goal
- Add validation coverage for live task identity, review-lane visibility, structured feedback actions, and replay-stable manual overrides.

Scope
- Fingerprint and duplicate-merge tests.
- Needs-review visibility and Current Tasks exclusion tests.
- Feedback artifact generation and ingestion tests.
- Structured triage actions such as confirm, dismiss, merge, snooze, and missed-task cases.
- Replay or rerun cases that prove overrides and feedback-sensitive behavior stay stable.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Validation covers task identity and duplicate handling across repeated or revised live inputs.
2. Needs-review visibility and Current Tasks exclusion rules are exercised directly.
3. Structured feedback actions and manual overrides round-trip cleanly through the feedback artifact.
4. Replay-sensitive cases prove that override state and feedback effects remain stable unless intentionally changed.
close_reason: Added e2e workflow tests: distinct tasks for identical body across source_type fingerprints; feedback override/manual_override_state stable after idempotent replay+reclassify; feedback draft YAML round-trip to confirm import for review-lane task.

title: Add locking, single-writer, budget, and performance-control tests for live sync
description:
Background
- AGENTS.md explicitly expects locking and single-writer tests, and the plan also calls for operational validation around retries, backpressure, and cost-aware behavior.
- After reviewing the open graph, this bead needs to be more explicit that operational validation is not just about pass or fail; it must also prove that the system emits enough structured diagnostics to understand why protective controls activated.

Goal
- Add validation coverage for locking, single-writer behavior, budget-aware fallback, performance controls, and the diagnostics that explain them.

Scope
- Locking and single-writer tests.
- Budget and heuristics-only fallback tests.
- Throughput-control tests for chunking, batching, backpressure, and load-shedding behavior.
- Operational failure-path cases where controls activate under stress.
- Assertions about detailed structured logs or artifacts that explain why protective behavior triggered.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Locking and single-writer behavior are validated under mixed live paths.
2. Budget-aware fallback and performance controls are exercised under realistic stress cases.
3. Operational failure-path cases verify that protective behavior is bounded and replay-safe.
4. Validation asserts that detailed structured diagnostics exist so operators can understand lock contention, fallback activation, and throughput throttling.
close_reason: Added tests/test_live_sync_controls.py: cross-thread drain vs RuntimeLock contention (second sqlite conn), canonical vault/var lock path alignment, pid line in lock file, lark-cli page-size 50 argv, bounded distill limit=200 via poll_once. Reduced flake in test_runtime cross-process drain wait (0.35s margin).

title: Preserve task-generation, task identity, and feedback-lane behavior in live sync
description:
Background
- The current live-sync bead set is strong on transport, document modeling, runtime mechanics, and migration honesty, but it does not yet make the task-generation trust boundary explicit enough.
- The project plan is not satisfied merely by pulling records into SQLite. Live mode must continue to honor deterministic heuristics first, configurable multilingual task cues, stable task identity, provenance retention, and a visible `needs-review` path for uncertain items.
- `AGENTS.md` and the plan both make it clear that the project is meant to preserve trust through explicit, reviewable behavior rather than hiding ambiguity. That means live sync must not bypass the review lane or silently create durable open-work items without the same lifecycle rules the offline flow is supposed to enforce.
- This feature exists to prevent a subtle but dangerous failure mode: a transport-complete live adapter that is still behaviorally incomplete because it shortcuts classification, fingerprinting, review visibility, or the human feedback loop.

Goal
- Preserve the canonical task-generation, task-identity, and structured-review contract when records arrive through live polling or events rather than replay-only fixture runs.

Scope
- Deterministic heuristics, configurable patterns, multilingual cues, and confidence bands in live mode.
- Stable task fingerprinting, merge rules, duplicate handling, and provenance retention across live surfaces.
- Explicit `needs-review` handling so uncertain live findings remain visible without polluting durable Current Tasks by default.
- Integration points between live-created candidates and the Markdown plus plannotator feedback workflow.

Why this is a separate feature track
- If this work is left implicit inside transport or runtime beads, it is too easy for implementation to accidentally say "live sync works" when only the ingestion side works.
- The plan's safety model depends on surfacing uncertain items visibly and preserving stable task identity over time, especially when repeated asks, edits, or mixed source classes show up in the same operational window.
- Future selves should treat this track as the answer to: "How do we know live sync still behaves like Lark-to-notes rather than just like a chat mirroring script?"
design:
Design principles
- Treat the review lane as part of the safety model, not as a secondary UX refinement.
- Keep heuristics-first behavior explicit so live sync can degrade gracefully when models are unavailable or too expensive.
- Make task identity stable across mixed intake paths and repeated sightings so the live adapter updates work instead of duplicating it.
- Preserve provenance from surfaced task back to canonical source context so operators can audit why an item appeared.

How this feature fits the larger system
- The shared intake ledger tells us what record we saw.
- This feature tells us what that record means as potential work.
- The rendering feature then decides where that surfaced meaning becomes visible in the vault.

Non-goals
- This feature does not create a second task lifecycle outside the canonical task system.
- It does not justify making live sync more aggressive than the planned review-lane model just because the system is continuous.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Live sync has an explicit heuristics-first classification contract with configurable multilingual cues, confidence bands, and clear LLM-escalation boundaries.
2. Stable task identity, duplicate-merge rules, and provenance retention are defined across chat, document, comment, and reply surfaces.
3. Needs-review handling is explicit: uncertain items remain visible for review and are not silently dropped or promoted into durable Current Tasks by default.
4. The Markdown plus plannotator feedback loop is connected to live-created candidates so false positives, false negatives, and policy improvements can flow back into the system.
notes:
Future-self reminder
- If a future implementation claims that live sync is complete while this feature is still weak or implicit, that claim is probably wrong. Transport without stable task meaning is not the product we are trying to build.
close_reason: All child tasks lw-m5k.8.1–8.6 and prerequisite lw-m5k.9 are closed; plan documents classification, fingerprinting, needs-review, feedback loop, triage semantics, and live quality metrics.

title: Define the live classification contract for deterministic heuristics, configurable patterns, and confidence bands
description:
Background
- The plan says first-pass detection should rely mainly on deterministic heuristics plus configuration, should support multilingual input, and should separate high-confidence tasks from lower-confidence candidates and `needs-review` items.
- The existing live-sync beads assume the canonical pipeline will handle classification, but they do not yet state what the live-specific contract is for confidence bands, configurable cues, and uncertainty handling.
- Without this task, live mode could quietly drift into a weaker rule set than replay mode, especially when incremental records arrive without the larger context that offline analysis may have.

Goal
- Define the live classification contract for deterministic heuristics, configurable patterns, multilingual cues, and confidence bands.

Scope
- Heuristics-first behavior for live records.
- Configurable English, Chinese, and mixed-language cues.
- Confidence-band outputs such as task, follow-up, context, and `needs-review`.
- Boundaries for when live processing may escalate to LLM assistance rather than relying on heuristics alone.

Why this matters
- This task protects the core product behavior: surfacing likely work without pretending uncertainty does not exist.
close_reason: Implemented configurable deterministic classification via reclassify CLI high/medium regex overrides and escalation-threshold control, with tests covering custom-rule behavior and invalid pattern validation.

title: Extend task fingerprinting, duplicate-merge rules, and provenance retention across live source classes
description:
Background
- The plan requires stable task fingerprints, merge rules, and provenance so repeated asks do not explode into duplicate work items.
- Live sync increases the pressure on these rules because repeated asks can appear across multiple runs, mixed intake paths, or mutable document threads.
- The current graph does not yet isolate the live-specific identity problem as a first-class design task.

Goal
- Extend stable task identity, duplicate-merge behavior, and provenance retention to live-created items across source classes.

Scope
- Task fingerprint inputs for chat, document, comment, and reply records.
- Merge or update rules for repeated asks and near-duplicate asks.
- Provenance retention from surfaced task back to canonical source context.
- Expectations for how live edits or later-arriving context affect an existing task rather than creating a new one.

Why this matters
- A live adapter that ingests correctly but duplicates work items would still fail the project's usefulness test.
close_reason: Completed identity/provenance hardening by including source_type in reclassify fingerprint inputs and attaching idempotent evidence on duplicate upserts so repeated asks preserve provenance without duplicate tasks.

title: Specify live needs-review visibility, daily-note review-lane behavior, and Current Tasks exclusion rules
description:
Background
- The plan is explicit that lower-confidence items should remain visible in a review lane instead of silently disappearing, and that `needs-review` items should stay out of Current Tasks by default.
- The present live-sync beads identify this as a missing area, but they do not yet spell out the concrete visibility rules for live-created uncertain items.
- This is a trust-boundary issue: if live sync hides uncertain items, operators miss work; if it promotes them too aggressively, Current Tasks becomes noisy and untrustworthy.

Goal
- Specify how live `needs-review` items remain visible, where they appear, and when they are intentionally excluded from durable open-work views.

Scope
- Daily-note review-lane behavior for live-created uncertain items.
- Exclusion rules for Current Tasks by default.
- Relationship between uncertainty state, operator review, and later promotion.
- Expectations for repeated sightings or edits to an existing `needs-review` candidate.

Why this matters
- This task translates the plan's review-lane philosophy into concrete live behavior instead of leaving it as a vague aspiration.
close_reason: Implemented explicit Current Tasks exclusion defaults for needs_review status/task_class while keeping those items visible through daily/review lanes, with item and batch render tests.

title: Connect live-created candidates to the Markdown plus plannotator feedback workflow
description:
Background
- The plan's feedback workflow depends on Markdown plus YAML artifacts and plannotator as the initial human review surface.
- The current live-adapter graph mentions feedback only indirectly, which leaves an important unanswered question: how do live-created candidates enter the same structured review loop the project expects for tuning and correction?
- If live sync bypasses this loop, the system loses one of its intended mechanisms for improving heuristics and correcting false positives or false negatives over time.

Goal
- Define how live-created uncertain items and surfaced tasks plug into the Markdown plus plannotator feedback workflow.

Scope
- Which live-created artifacts should feed review outputs.
- How structured triage actions and optional comments map back into the live pipeline.
- How feedback affects tuning inputs, policy revisions, or future reclassification.
- Operator expectations for reviewing live-created candidates versus replay-generated candidates.

Why this matters
- This is the bead that keeps the live adapter connected to the project's intended human-in-the-loop refinement model.
close_reason: Documented live-created candidates vs replay: same Markdown/plannotator path, feedback import, tuning, and operator expectations in plan_for_lark_to_notes_workflow.md

title: Define structured triage actions, manual overrides, and replay-stable feedback artifact semantics
description:
Background
- The plan phase and exit criteria for feedback go beyond generic plannotator integration. They explicitly require confirmations, dismissals, merges, snoozes, missed-task actions, and persistence of manual overrides across replay unless intentionally reset.
- The current live-sync backlog mentions the feedback workflow, but until this bead exists, the action vocabulary and replay-stability rules for feedback remain too implicit.

Goal
- Define the structured triage actions, manual override semantics, and replay-stable feedback artifact behavior for live-created candidates and surfaced tasks.

Scope
- Structured actions such as confirm, dismiss, merge, snooze, and missed-task or false-negative reporting.
- How those actions are represented in the Markdown plus YAML feedback artifact.
- How manual overrides persist across replay, reclassification, and later live sightings unless intentionally changed.
- Boundaries between operator override state, automatic task lifecycle logic, and future policy updates.

Why this matters
- This task closes an important trust gap: without explicit round-trip semantics, feedback looks supported in theory but remains unreliable in practice.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The live feedback design includes an explicit action vocabulary for confirm, dismiss, merge, snooze, and missed-task cases.
2. Feedback artifact semantics are clear enough to round-trip these actions without ambiguity.
3. Manual overrides and operator decisions persist across replay and reclassification unless intentionally reset.
4. The resulting design is detailed enough to drive both implementation and focused validation.
notes:
Future-self reminder
- If the system cannot preserve a human correction across replay, then the feedback loop is not trustworthy no matter how pretty the review UI looks.
close_reason: Completed by making missed_task artifacts explicit for replay, defaulting promotion_rec from task_class for confirm and missed_task semantics, locking those behaviors with feedback tests, and documenting the per-target feedback contract in README.

title: Track duplicate, confirm, dismiss, and missed-task quality metrics for live refinement
description:
Background
- Phase 7 of the plan explicitly calls for tracking duplicate rate, dismiss rate, and confirm rate over time so the heuristics and review flow can be refined intentionally rather than by gut feel.
- The current live graph covers feedback semantics and validation, but it still does not give quality-measurement and calibration signals a first-class bead.
- Without that explicit task, the system risks collecting feedback without turning it into observable quality metrics that help users decide whether the automation is actually improving.

Goal
- Define the feedback-derived quality metrics and calibration signals that live sync should track over time.

Scope
- Duplicate rate, dismiss rate, confirm rate, and missed-task or false-negative signals.
- How these metrics relate to source class, policy version, and review-lane outcomes.
- Boundaries between per-run metrics, rolling trend metrics, and operator-facing summaries.
- How these signals should inform later heuristics, escalation thresholds, and policy refinement.

Why this matters
- This task closes the loop between human review and measurable quality improvement, which is a key part of making the system better for users over time.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The live design explicitly includes duplicate rate, confirm rate, dismiss rate, and missed-task or false-negative signals.
2. These metrics are related back to source class, policy version, and review outcomes strongly enough to support later tuning.
3. The bead distinguishes between per-run, rolling, and operator-visible summary views of quality.
4. The result is actionable enough to guide both implementation and later calibration work.
close_reason: Completed scoped quality views by adding per-import run-like rollups, rolling 7d/30d summaries, daily trend buckets, and operator-visible CLI reporting with validated tests.

title: Formalize shared intake ledger, cursor, and idempotency semantics for live surfaces
description:
Background
- The plan is explicit that polling and event-driven intake must converge on one logical stream with canonical ingest keys, durable raw capture, per-source cursors, replayability, and downstream stage tracking.
- The current live-sync graph mentions normalization and dedup in several places, but it does not yet isolate the shared-ledger and idempotency contract as its own first-class design problem.
- That missing layer is dangerous because transport can look complete while the system still lacks a clear answer to basic questions such as: what makes two sightings the same record, what counts as a new revision, how do stage completions get tracked, and how do replay and reclassification avoid double-writing derived artifacts?
- This feature exists to capture the central dataflow contract that all live surfaces must obey before chat, document, runtime, or rendering work can honestly claim correctness.

Goal
- Define and harden the shared intake-ledger, cursor, and idempotency semantics that let live polling, event intake, replay, and reclassification converge on one canonical pipeline.

Scope
- Canonical ingest-key rules across messages, documents, comments, replies, and revisions.
- Ledger fields such as first-seen, last-seen, latest revision, processing state, intake path, and stage completion.
- Cursor and checkpoint behavior per source stream.
- Replay, reclassification, dedup, and content-hash skip semantics for live data.

Why this is a separate feature track
- Without this contract, every other feature has to guess what "the same record" means.
- The project's claims about replay, idempotency, auditability, and safe retries all rest on this layer whether or not it is named explicitly.
- Future selves should treat this as the backbone that makes the rest of the live adapter more than a collection of subprocess calls.
design:
Design principles
